Section 1.11
CURVED ARROWS, ARROW PUSHING, AND CHEMICAL REACTIONS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Curved Arrows as Electron Bookkeeping
Organic chemists use curved arrows to show the movement of electrons in chemical reactions
A full-headed arrow implies movement of a pair of electrons; arrow starts at the source and ends at the sink
For example, dissociation of a molecule AB is drawn as
Note the changes in electron positions and formal charges implied by the curved arrow.
Examples of dissociation all follow this pattern

Multiple Curved Arrows
A bond may break due to electron donation from another source
In this case we use multiple curved arrows to show the flow of electrons from source to sink
Note the changes in bonding pattern and formal charge in the elementary step below
Proton transfer processes are associated with similar electron flow

Multiple Bonds and Curved Arrows
A curved arrow beginning at a multiple bond implies movement of only two of the electrons in the bond
The σ bond remains intact while the π electrons move
Only one of the two atoms of the π bond holds onto the pair of electrons
π bonds can act as electron sources…
…or electron sinks!

Section 1.12
ACIDS AND BASES: THE BRØNSTED-LOWRY VIEW

Brønsted-Lowry Acids and Bases
Acid: a species that donates a proton to a base
Base: a species that accepts a proton from an acid
Species related by the addition or removal of a single proton are called conjugates
When an acid surrenders a proton in an acid-base reaction, its conjugate base is formed
When a base gains a proton in an acid-base reaction, its conjugate acid is formed
Note the curved arrows used to show the flow of electrons and the changes in formal charge as the reaction occurs.

The Acid Dissociation Constant
Water reacts with an acid HA to form H 3 O + and A −
The equilibrium constant for this process is called the acid dissociation constant ( K a )
The larger K a is, the stronger the acid
K a for an acid is related to K b for the conjugate base of the acid

p k a : A Brain-friendly Acidity Constant
k a values span a massive range and are usually far less than 1, so we usually work with the negative logarithm of k a or p k a
Thanks to the negative sign, a smaller p k a is associated with a stronger acid

Acidity Constants ( p K a ) of Acids
What structural factors do the strong acids have in common?
How does acidity change as structure changes?
Acidity Constants ( p K a ) of Acids-1

Section 1.13
HOW STRUCTURE AFFECTS ACID STRENGTH

Structural Factors Influencing Acidity
There are four structural factors that exert a profound influence on the acidity of a proton
The strength of the bond to the atom from which the proton is lost
The electronegativity of the atom from which the proton is lost
Electron delocalization in the conjugate base
When comparing the relative strengths of two acids, we focus on how they differ on these three factors

Factor 1: H–A Bond Strength
As the size of the atom to which H is bonded increases , the H–A bond strength decreases and acidity increases
Greater polarizability of larger conjugate base anions also contributes to greater acidity
The hydrohalic acids illustrate this effect nicely

Factor 2: Electronegativity of A in H–A
As the electronegativity of A increases , the polarization of the H–A bond increases and acidity increases
The partial positive charge on H increases, increasing the likelihood that it will be transferred as H + (i.e., HA will act as an acid)
The covalent hydrides of second-row nonmetals illustrate this effect nicely. Acidity increases moving to the right in the periodic table

Factor 3: Inductive Effects
Nearby electronegative atoms can have a similar effect on the acidity of a proton. As the number of nearby electronegative atoms increases, acidity increases
Electronegative atoms pull electron density towards themselves, increasing the partial positive charge on H
This “pulling” is called an inductive effect or induction
Trifluoroethanol is a considerably stronger acid (about 10,000 times) than ethanol due to inductive effects of the fluorine atoms .

Resonance Delocalization
Loss of a proton establishes a lone pair on the conjugate base. When this lone pair is involved in resonance delocalization, the conjugate base is stabilized
The acid is more acidic ( ∆ G degree for loss of the proton is more negative) when the conjugate base is resonance stabilized
For example, acetic acid (CH 3 CO 2 H, p K a 4.75) is much more acidic than ethanol (CH 3 CH 2 OH, p K a 16)
The conjugate base of acetic acid (CH 3 CO 2 − ) is stabilized by resonance but the conjugate base of ethanol (CH 3 CH 2 O − ) is not.

Section 1.14
ACID-BASE EQUILIBRIA

Acid-base Equilibria Favor the Weak
The equilibrium constant for a generic acid base reaction can be calculated from the K a values for HA (reactant acid) and HB + (product acid)
Because a reaction favors products when K > 1 and vice versa , acid-base equilibria favor the side with the weaker acid (higher p K a !)

Acid-base Equilibria Favor the Weak-1
Because a reaction favors products when K > 1 and vice versa , acid-base equilibria favor the side with the weaker acid (lower p K a !)

Acid-base Equilibria Favor the Weak-2
Because a reaction favors products when K > 1 and vice versa , acid-base equilibria favor the side with the weaker acid (lower p K a !)

Acid-base Equilibria Favor the Weak-3
Because a reaction favors products when K > 1 and vice versa , acid-base equilibria favor the side with the weaker acid (lower p K a !)
From p K a values for HA and HB + alone, one can predict the favored side of an acid base equilibrium.

Section 1.15
ACIDS AND BASES: THE LEWIS VIEW

Lewis Theory of Acids and Bases
Lewis Acid: a species that accepts an electron pair from a base
Lewis Base: a species that donates an electron pair to an acid
Brønsted acids and bases are subsets of Lewis acids and bases respectively!

Example: BF 3 + OEt 2
Diethyl ether donates a pair of electrons from oxygen and thus serves as a Lewis base or nucleophile
Boron trifluoride accepts the pair of electrons from diethyl ether at boron and thus serves as a Lewis acid or electrophile
A new bond forms between oxygen and boron, the electrons of which came from oxygen (the Lewis base)

Importance of Lewis Theory
Lewis acids ( electrophiles ) and bases ( nucleophiles ) abound in organic chemistry
Partially or fully negative atoms often act as nucleophiles; partially or fully positive atoms often act as electrophiles
Every polar reaction can be envisioned as an exchange of electrons between nucleophiles and electrophiles
Some of the structural factors that affect Brønsted acid/base strength also affect Lewis acid/base strength
